content.text: Feature Sunset Communication and Migration Plan

Sunsetting a GenAI feature — removing an inference endpoint, deprecating a prompt template API, shutting down a model that has been superseded — requires more than setting a removal date. Users who depend on the feature need time to understand the change, plan the migration, and execute it. Without structured communication, a feature sunset generates support escalations, broken integrations, and customer loss that is disproportionate to the technical scope of the removal.

A feature sunset communication plan coordinates four elements: the message (what is changing, why, and what users should do), the timeline (how much notice is provided and when each communication fires), the audience segmentation (external users, internal services, support teams), and the migration plan (how to move from the sunsetted feature to the replacement).

attrs.alt: Feature sunset communication timeline: T-90 initial notice, T-60 in-product signals, T-30 migration reminders, T-7 final warning, T-0 sunset — across external users, internal teams, and support lanes

content.text: Not every underused feature should be sunsetted immediately. The decision to sunset requires weighing the cost of maintaining the feature against the migration burden on users. For GenAI features, the cost calculation includes GPU compute cost for running models that serve the feature, operational overhead of keeping old model versions current with security patches, and engineering overhead of maintaining compatibility with the deprecated API surface.
Replacement covers all documented use cases
Feature requires a dependency that is not patchable
Feature no longer aligns with product direction
Feature is a differentiator or contractual commitment
Support burden is low relative to value delivered
Sunset the feature, not the users.

The decision to sunset should be made when maintaining the feature costs more than the value it delivers — but the communication plan treats the migration burden on users as a first-class concern, not an afterthought.
The 90-day notice provides the maximum time for users to plan migrations. It covers all communication channels simultaneously:
Email to all active users of the feature
(anyone who has called the endpoint or used the API in the past 180 days)
to engineering teams and support/success teams
and documentation page update marking the feature as deprecated

The T-90 notice must include the specific sunset date, the replacement feature or API, and a link to the migration guide. Vague notices ('this feature will be removed in the future') generate support tickets and mistrust; specific dates make the timeline actionable.
At T-60 days, the deprecated feature begins signaling its upcoming removal at the point of use — not just in external communications.

# app/middleware/deprecation_headers.py
# Adds Deprecation and Sunset headers to responses from deprecated endpoints

from datetime import date
from fastapi import Request, Response
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute

DEPRECATED_ENDPOINTS = {
    "/v1/completions": {
        "sunset_date": date(2026, 3, 1),
        "replacement": "/v1/chat/completions",
        "migration_guide": "https://docs.example.com/migration/completions-to-chat",
    },
    "/v1/models/davinci-002/generate": {
        "sunset_date": date(2026, 1, 15),
        "replacement": "/v1/models/gpt-4o-mini/generate",
        "migration_guide": "https://docs.example.com/migration/davinci-to-gpt4o",
    },
}

async def deprecation_middleware(request: Request, call_next):
    response: Response = await call_next(request)

    path = request.url.path
    if path in DEPRECATED_ENDPOINTS:
        info = DEPRECATED_ENDPOINTS[path]
        sunset_date = info["sunset_date"]
        days_remaining = (sunset_date - date.today()).days

        # RFC 8594 Sunset header — the date the resource will be removed
        response.headers["Sunset"] = sunset_date.strftime("%a, %d %b %Y 00:00:00 GMT")
        # RFC 8594 Deprecation header
        response.headers["Deprecation"] = "true"
        # Link to migration guide
        response.headers["Link"] = (
            f'<{info["migration_guide"]}>; rel="successor-version"'
        )
        # Custom header with days remaining (useful for automated monitoring)
        response.headers["X-Sunset-Days-Remaining"] = str(max(0, days_remaining))

    return response

For an SDK method, the deprecation signal is emitted via a  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'DeprecationWarning', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]} {'type': 'text', 'text': ' that surfaces in user logs and IDE tools:'}
The T-30 reminder targets users who have not yet migrated. This requires identifying who is still calling the deprecated endpoint or using the deprecated API:

# scripts/sunset_migration_status.py
# Generates a per-user migration status report at T-30

import datetime
from analytics import query_usage

SUNSET_DATE = datetime.date(2026, 3, 1)
DEPRECATED_ENDPOINT = "/v1/completions"
REPLACEMENT_ENDPOINT = "/v1/chat/completions"

def get_migration_status() -> list[dict]:
    # Users active on deprecated endpoint in the past 30 days
    deprecated_users = set(
        query_usage(endpoint=DEPRECATED_ENDPOINT, days=30, return_field="user_id")
    )
    # Of those, who has also adopted the replacement endpoint
    migrating_users = set(
        query_usage(endpoint=REPLACEMENT_ENDPOINT, days=30, return_field="user_id")
    ) & deprecated_users

    return [
        {
            "user_id": uid,
            "status": "migrating" if uid in migrating_users else "not-started",
            "days_remaining": (SUNSET_DATE - datetime.date.today()).days,
        }
        for uid in deprecated_users
    ]

def send_migration_reminders(statuses: list[dict]) -> None:
    not_started = [s for s in statuses if s["status"] == "not-started"]
    print(f"Sending T-30 reminders to {len(not_started)} users who have not started migration")
    for status in not_started:
        send_email(
            to=status["user_id"],
            template="sunset_t30_reminder",
            context={
                "days_remaining": status["days_remaining"],
                "sunset_date": SUNSET_DATE.isoformat(),
                "migration_guide_url": "https://docs.example.com/migration/completions-to-chat",
            }
        )

Users who are already migrating (calling both the deprecated and replacement endpoint) receive a different, lighter reminder that acknowledges their progress and encourages them to complete the migration.

The communication plan announces the sunset; the migration plan defines how users move from the old feature to the replacement. A migration plan is specific to the feature transition, not generic advice.
Specific API signature, response format, or behavior differences between old and new
The engineering team that owns both old and new
Ordered steps to update calling code, including code examples for the 3-5 most common use patterns
Where possible: a script or codemod that rewrites the old calling pattern to the new one
A temporary adapter that lets users adopt the new API endpoint while keeping their old request/response format during the migration period
Answers to the questions support receives most — 'does the new API cost more?', 'does it behave the same on edge input X?'
Example Migration Guide: Completions to Chat API

# Migration Guide: /v1/completions → /v1/chat/completions

**Sunset date**: 2026-03-01
**Reason**: The Completions API returns a single text continuation; the Chat API
supports multi-turn conversations, system prompts, and all current models.

## What changes

| Aspect | Old (/v1/completions) | New (/v1/chat/completions) |
|---|---|---|
| Request format | `{"prompt": "...", "max_tokens": 100}` | `{"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "..."}], "max_tokens": 100}` |
| Response field | `choices[0].text` | `choices[0].message.content` |
| System prompt | Not supported | `{"role": "system", "content": "..."}` as first message |
| Token counting | `usage.total_tokens` | Same — no change |

## Step-by-step migration

1. Replace the endpoint URL:
   ```
   # Old
   POST /v1/completions
   # New
   POST /v1/chat/completions
   ```

2. Update the request body:
   ```python
   # Old
   data = {"model": "davinci-002", "prompt": user_input, "max_tokens": 200}
   # New
   data = {"model": "gpt-4o-mini", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": user_input}], "max_tokens": 200}
   ```

3. Update the response parsing:
   ```python
   # Old
   result = response["choices"][0]["text"]
   # New
   result = response["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]
   ```

## Automated migration tool

Run to automatically update your codebase:
```bash
pip install genai-migrate
genai-migrate completions-to-chat --path ./src/
```

For users who cannot migrate immediately, a compatibility shim accepts the old request format and translates it to the new API internally. The shim is available from T-90 to T-30 (it is removed at T-30 to push users to complete the migration):

# app/shim/completions_shim.py
# Temporary compatibility shim: accepts old /v1/completions format,
# translates to /v1/chat/completions internally.
# REMOVAL DATE: T-30 (2026-02-01) — remove this file and its route on that date.

from fastapi import APIRouter, Request
import httpx

router = APIRouter()

@router.post("/v1/completions")
async def completions_shim(request: Request):
    body = await request.json()

    # Translate old format to new format
    chat_body = {
        "model": body.get("model", "gpt-4o-mini"),
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": body["prompt"]}],
        "max_tokens": body.get("max_tokens", 256),
        "temperature": body.get("temperature", 1.0),
    }

    # Forward to the real chat endpoint
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        resp = await client.post(
            "http://localhost/v1/chat/completions",
            json=chat_body,
            headers={"Authorization": request.headers.get("Authorization", "")},
        )
    chat_response = resp.json()

    # Translate response back to old format
    return {
        "id": chat_response["id"],
        "object": "text_completion",
        "choices": [
            {
                "text": chat_response["choices"][0]["message"]["content"],
                "index": 0,
                "finish_reason": chat_response["choices"][0]["finish_reason"],
            }
        ],
        "usage": chat_response["usage"],
    }

A sunset that completes cleanly has a migration completion dashboard that shows usage of the deprecated endpoint declining to zero before the sunset date. The dashboard drives decisions about whether to accelerate the timeline (if adoption is faster than expected) or extend it (if a significant user segment is blocked):
Escalate to account team for enterprise users
Investigate stalled migrators; offer direct help
On the sunset date itself, the feature removal is executed as a planned operational change, not an emergency:
24 hours before sunset to document any remaining non-migrated users for post-sunset support triage.
Enable maintenance mode on the deprecated endpoint
— return 410 Gone with a redirect link for 7 days before removing the route entirely.
Monitor for unexpected spikes in errors
in the 24 hours after sunset — these indicate undiscovered dependencies that missed the communication.
Assign an on-call engineer for 48 hours post-sunset
to handle urgent escalations from users who were unable to migrate.

A feature sunset communication plan converts a product removal decision into a structured, predictable process for users and internal teams. The four-phase timeline — initial notice at T-90, in-product signals at T-60, deadline reminder at T-30, final warning at T-7 — provides the right cadence of communication without overwhelming users or creating false urgency too early.

The migration plan is the substance behind the communication: without a specific, step-by-step guide and ideally an automated migration tool, a sunset announcement is just a deletion notice. Users who are given a clear path from the old feature to the new one — with code examples, a compatibility shim for the transition period, and direct support during the migration window — complete migrations faster and with fewer escalations.

For GenAI features specifically, the migration plan must address cases where the old and new model or API do not produce identical outputs. Users who have built systems that depend on specific response characteristics (response format, refusal behavior, token count) need explicit guidance on what to test and how to validate that the replacement meets their requirements before switching over.
